1: wĕʿattāh śāḥăqû ʿālay — 'but now those younger than me mock me' — śāḥaq (to laugh/mock, from the same root as Isaac's name) marks the exact inversion of Job 29: there Job was honored by elders and princes; here he is mocked by the young of the lowest social tier. ʾăšer māʾastî ʾăḇôṯām lāšîṯ ʿim-kalbê ṣōʾnî — 'whose fathers I would have disdained to set with the dogs of my flock' — keleḇ (dog) in the ancient Near East was not a pet but a scavenger, a term of contempt (1 Samuel 17:43; 2 Samuel 9:8); guarding sheep with such men would have been below the dignity of Job's household. The social descent described is vertiginous: from revered elder (Job 29) to the mockery of society's rejects.
2: gam-kōaḥ yĕḏêhem lāmmāh lî — 'what use to me is the strength of their hands?' — lāmmāh lî = 'what is it to me?'; kōaḥ yāḏ = the strength/capacity of the hand. ʿālêmô ʾāḇad kālaḥ — 'upon them old age has perished' — kālaḥ is a rare word (only here and Job 5:26) meaning full age or mature strength; the phrase says that vigor has already wasted away on these men. Job's contempt is not for poverty itself but for those who lack even the minimal productive capacity that might make them members of his community.
3: bĕḥeser ûḇĕrāʿāḇ galmûḏ — 'through want and famine they are barren/solitary' — galmûḏ means barren or desolate (used in Isaiah 49:21 of a barren woman); here applied to people who have been left without community or offspring in the wilderness of desolation. hāʿōrĕqîm ṣiyyāh ʾemeš — 'gnawing at waste and desolation in former times' — ṣiyyāh = drought/desolation (the wilderness noun); the image is of people driven to gnaw at desolation itself, the wilderness as the only food available.
4: haqqōṭĕpîm mallûaḥ ʿălê-śîaḥ — 'those who pluck saltbush leaves from among the shrubs' — mallûaḥ is the orache or saltbush (Atriplex), an edible but unpalatable shrub; śîaḥ = shrub/bush. wĕšōreš rĕṯāmîm laḥmām — 'the root of the broom plant is their food' — rōṯem (broom plant, Retama raetam) is used as fuel in Psalms 120:4 and 1 Kings 19:4-5; digging its roots for food marks extreme destitution. The two plants together — saltbush leaves and broom roots — mark a diet below any tolerable subsistence level.
5: min-gēw yĕgōrāšû — 'they are driven out from human society' — gēw (the midst of community, the middle); yĕgōrāšû from gāraš (to expel/drive away, the verb of divorcing and expelling — Genesis 4:14 uses it for Cain's expulsion). yirʾû-bô yārîʿû kĕgannāḇ — 'people shout after them as after a thief' — the hue-and-cry against a thief (Exodus 22:2-3 describes the thief caught in the act) applied to these wanderers; they are treated as criminals by their mere presence.
6: lišḵōn bĕnaḥălê nĕḥālîm — 'to dwell in the gullies of ravines' — naḥal (wadi/ravine) + the diminutive nĕḥālîm forms 'gullies within ravines' — the most remote and inhospitable terrain. ḥōrê ʿāpār wĕkêpîm — 'holes of the earth and among the rock-crevices' — ḥōr = hole/burrow; kêp̄ = craggy rock surface; the imagery of people forced underground into animal burrows. The same vocabulary governs Hebrews 11:38's description of OT heroes who 'wandered in deserts and mountains, and in caves and holes in the ground.'
7: bên-śîḥîm yinhāqû — 'among the bushes they bray' — nāhaq is the verb for a donkey braying (Job 27:1); applied to people it is deeply dehumanizing. taḥat ḥărûl yĕsuppāḵû — 'beneath the nettles they are gathered together' — ḥārûl = thorn/nettle plant; sāpaḵ (to gather/pile); they huddle under thorn plants. The verse pairs donkey-braying with thorn-sheltering as the final images of sub-animal existence.
8: bĕnê-nāḇāl gam-bĕnê bĕlî-šēm — 'children of the base fool, children of namelessness' — nāḇāl is the biblical word for the moral-intellectual fool who denies God (Psalms 14:1 'the nāḇāl says in his heart there is no God'; Proverbs 17:21; 1 Samuel 25:25 where Nabal's name matches his character). bĕlî-šēm — 'without a name/without reputation' — to be without šēm (name) in the ancient world is to be without social identity, honor, or perpetuation; the worst state short of non-existence. nikkĕʾû min-hāʾāreṣ — 'they were flogged/driven out of the land' — nāḵāʾ (to smite/beat); the passive describes expulsion by force.
9: wĕʿattāh nĕgînāṯām hāyîṯî — 'and now I have become their song' — nĕgînāh is a musical-instrument piece or mocking song; in Lamentations 3:14 the same word describes the speaker becoming 'the song of all the people.' wāʾĕhî lāhem lĕmillāh — 'and I have become a byword to them' — millāh is word/byword/proverb; the once-honored patron becomes the content of their mockery. The full reversal of Job 29's honor is now explicit: the one who commanded respect is now entertainment for social rejects.
10: tĕʿāḇûnî rāḥăqû mimmennî — 'they abhor me and keep their distance' — tāʿab (to abominate/make detestable, a strong disgust-word used in Psalms 5:6; 14:1 for God's rejection of evil); the same root that governs Job's self-description in Job 9:31. ûmippānay lō-ḥāśĕkû rōq — 'and they do not withhold spitting in my face' — rōq = saliva/spitting; spitting was the ultimate act of contempt (Numbers 12:14; Deuteronomy 25:9; Isaiah 50:6 where the Servant has his face spat upon). The connection between Job's suffering and the Servant of Isaiah 50:6 (who 'did not hide his face from insult and spitting') is one of the chapter's most significant poetic echoes.
11: kî yitrô pittaḥ wayyĕʿannēnî — 'because he has loosed his cord and afflicted me' — yeter (cord/string, used for a bow-string or tent-cord); loosing it = releasing the tension that previously kept things in check. The subject is disputed: is it God who loosed Job's cord (his restraint), or has Job's affliction itself been loosed? The MKT renders this as God loosening the restraint over Job's mockers. wĕriṣṣāh šillĕḥû mippānāy — 'and they cast off all restraint in my presence' — šillēaḥ + riṣṣāh (the bridle or restraint); once God withdraws the protective force that commanded respect, every social inhibition against mistreating Job is removed.
12: ʿal-yāmîn pirhāḥ yāqûmû — 'on my right the rabble rises up' — pirhāḥ is a rare word (only here) often understood as 'brood/mob' or 'rabble'; it may derive from pāraḥ (to sprout/spread). riglay šillēḥû wayyāsōllû ʿālay — 'they push aside my feet and build their roads of ruin against me' — rŏgel (foot/leg); sālaʿ (to mound/build a siege-ramp); the military metaphor of a siege is applied to the mob: they are constructing an assault route against Job.
13: nāṯĕsû nĕṯîḇāṯî — 'they wreck my path' — nāṯaś (to pull down, used of demolishing walls in 2 Kings 25:10); nĕṯîḇāh = path/course. lĕhawwāṯî yôʿîlû — 'they advance my downfall' — yāʿal (to profit/advance) used ironically: their 'profit' is Job's destruction; hawwāh = downfall/calamity (used in Psalms 5:9; Job 38:12; Job 52:2). lō ʿōzēr lāmô — 'with no one to help them' — they accomplish Job's destruction without even needing outside assistance; the passivity of isolation is underscored.
14: kĕp̄ereṣ rāḥāḇ yĕḇōʾû — 'they come in through a wide breach' — pereṣ (breach in a wall, used in 2 Samuel 5:20 and Amos 4:3 for military breaches); the wide-breach image continues the siege metaphor. taḥat šōʾāh hiṯgalgālû — 'they rush in amid the ruin/catastrophe' — šōʾāh (catastrophe/devastation, used in Job 38:15; Proverbs 1:27; Zephaniah 1:15 for divine judgment); hitgalgal (to roll/rush in). The entire passage (vv12-14) uses warfare imagery to describe social abandonment as a military assault.
15: hāhpak ʿālay ballāhôṯ — 'terrors have been turned loose against me' — ballāhôṯ (plural of ballāhāh, terrors/sudden frights) is a key Job term (Job 9:34; Job 13:21; Job 18:11,14; Job 24:17); pl. indicates overwhelming multiplicity. tirrōdep̄ kārûaḥ nĕḏîḇāṯî — 'my dignity has been chased away like the wind' — nāḏîḇāh (dignity/princely quality, from nāḏîḇ = noble); the wind-chasing image for something ungraspably gone. kĕʿāḇ ʿāḇĕrāh yĕšûʿāṯî — 'my prosperity has passed away like a cloud' — yĕšûʿāh (deliverance/prosperity) normally used for divine salvation (cf. Psalms 3:2; Isaiah 12:2 'God is my yĕšûʿāh'); its application to social/material prosperity shows how completely Job's world has been the arena of divine blessing.
16: wĕʿattāh ʿālay tiššāpēḵ nap̄šî — 'and now my soul is poured out upon me' — šāpak (to pour out, used of blood: Genesis 9:6; and of water: 2 Samuel 14:14); nepeš (soul/life) poured out like a liquid. This exact construction — nep̄eš + šāpak + ʿāl — appears in Psalms 22:14 ('I am poured out like water... my heart has melted like wax'); the Psalm of dereliction uses the same self-dissolution language Job uses here. yōʾḥăzûnî yĕmê ʿānî — 'days of affliction have seized me' — ʿŏnî (affliction/poverty from ʿānāh, to be afflicted); the days of affliction as a grasping agent, seizing Job in their grip.
17: laylāh ʿaṣmōṯay niqar mēʿālay — 'at night my bones are racked/bored through' — nāqar (to bore/pierce, used in 2 Kings 25:7 for boring out eyes); the night pain gnaws at Job's skeletal structure. wĕʿōrĕqay lō yiškāḇû — 'and the gnawing pain does not rest' — ʿōreq (from nāqar? or from ʿāraḵ = to gnaw?) — the pain as a living thing that does not sleep even when Job tries to; the sleeplessness of chronic pain given poetic form.
18: bĕrāḇ-kōaḥ yitḥappēś lĕḇûšî — 'with great force my garment is seized' — ḥāp̄aś (to disguise/search through; in the hitpa'el to grip/hold tightly). The garment-seizing by illness: disease literally grabs and distorts Job's clothing, twisting it around him. kĕp̄î kuttontî yaʾăzrēnî — 'it binds me like the collar of my tunic' — ketonnet (the long garment/tunic; the same word as Joseph's coat in Genesis 37:3); the collar (pî = mouth/opening) becomes a strangling grip. Disease as a garment-seizing assailant is a striking pathological image.
19: hōrānî laḥōmer — 'he has thrown me into the mud' — yārāh in the hip'il = to throw; ḥōmer = clay/mud; to be thrown into mud is to be reduced to raw material. wāʾetmassēl kĕʿāpār wāʾēp̄er — 'and I have become like dust and ashes' — māšal (to be like, to resemble); ʿāpār wāʾēp̄er — the dust-and-ashes pair is the standard OT expression for the absolute lowness of human creaturely existence (Genesis 18:27; Job 42:6); to become the metaphor itself is the deepest descent.
20: ʾeśāʿaq ʾēlêḵā wĕlō taʿănēnî — 'I cry out to you and you do not answer me' — šāʿaq (to cry out in distress, the verb of desperation); the divine non-answer as active theological abandonment. This is the direct parallel to Psalms 22:2 ('I cry by day and you do not answer'). ʿāmadtî wattitbōnēn bî — 'I stand and you only stare at me' — bîn (to understand/perceive) in the hitpo'el = to stare with incomprehension; God is portrayed as observing without responding. The combination of the divine silence motif and the divine-gaze motif frames Job's abandonment experience in terms almost identical to the messianic Psalm of dereliction.
21: hēhāp̄aḵ lĕʿākzār lî — 'you have become cruel to me' — ʿakzār (cruel/savage, used in Proverbs 5:9; Isaiah 13:9 for God's judgment-day cruelty against enemies); Job is experiencing the treatment reserved for enemies applied to himself. bĕʿōṣem yāḏĕḵā tiśṭĕmēnî — 'with the power of your hand you persecute me' — śāṭam (to oppose/be hostile to; cf. Genesis 27:41, Esau's hostility; and Satan's root in this stem in 1:6 interpretations); the divine hand as adversarial rather than protective.
22: tiśśāʾēnî ʾel-rûaḥ tarkîḇēnî — 'you lift me up to the wind; you cause me to ride it' — rāḵaḇ (to ride, used in Deuteronomy 33:26 for God who rides the heavens); Job being made to ride the wind = total disorientation and helplessness. wĕtĕmōgĕgēnî tûšiyyāh — 'and you dissolve my substance/effective power' — mûg (to melt/dissolve); tûšiyyāh (the distinctive Job word for effective wisdom/substance, also in Job 6:13; Job 11:6; Job 12:16; Job 26:3; Proverbs 8:14); Job's capacity for purposeful action is melted away by God's storm-riding.
23: kî-yāḏaʿtî māwet tĕšîḇēnî — 'for I know you will bring me to death' — the only certainty left is that God will direct Job toward death. wĕḇêṯ môʿēḏ lĕḵol-ḥāy — 'to the house appointed for all living' — bêṯ môʿēḏ is a remarkable compound: bêṯ (house) + môʿēḏ (the term for the divinely appointed Tabernacle/Temple meeting-place, as in ʾōhel môʿēḏ = tent of meeting). Death is called a 'house of appointment/meeting'; the irony that the Tabernacle-meeting language — the locus of divine-human encounter — should be applied to death is bitter and theologically charged.
24: ʾaḵ lō-bĕʿî yišlaḥ yāḏ — 'yet does not one in a ruin reach out his hand?' — the verse is difficult; the sense seems to be: even in a heap of ruins, people reach out and cry for help. Job is asserting the universal practice of crying for help in disaster as the ground for his own cries. The appeal to common human practice challenges any theology that would silence the sufferer's cry.
25: ʾim-lō bāḵîṯî lĕqĕšēh-yôm — 'did I not weep for those in trouble?' — qāšâ yôm = hard/difficult day, the day of hardship; Job's past compassion for others in distress. ʾāgĕlāh nap̄šî lāʾeḇyôn — 'was not my soul grieved for the poor?' — ʾāgal (to have grief/sorrow, related to Arabic root for deep interior distress); nepeš + ʾāgal for the poor echoes the socially engaged righteousness of Job 29. Having wept for others' distress, Job expects (and receives instead) divine indifference to his own.
26: kî ṭôḇ qiwwîṯî wayyāḇōʾ rāʿ — 'when I looked for good, evil came' — the bitterest reversal formula: qiwwîṯî (I hoped/waited, the verb of tiqwāh, hope) for ṭôḇ (good) but rāʿ (evil) came. The language of hope-and-disappointment is applied not to a single event but to the whole shape of Job's life after his integrity. wăʾăyahel lāʾôr wayyāḇōʾ ʾōp̄el — 'and when I waited for light, thick darkness came' — ʾōp̄el (thick darkness, the same darkness of the inward parts of Job 3:6 and Proverbs 7:9); the antithesis of hoped-for light arriving as its opposite.
27: mēʿay ḥārû wĕlō-ḏāmû — 'my inner parts churn and do not rest' — ḥārāh (to burn/ferment; applied to the gut here as visceral turmoil); the meʿāyim (intestines/inner parts) as the seat of emotion mirrors Jeremiah 4:19 ('my bowels, my bowels! I am pained at my very heart') and Lamentations 1:20. qiddĕmûnî yĕmê ʿānî — 'days of affliction have overtaken/confronted me' — qāḏam (to come before/confront, used in Psalms 18:18 of enemies who came against); the days of affliction as active assailants meeting Job head-on.
28: qōḏēr hillaḵtî bĕlō ḥammāh — 'I go about in mourning without the sun' — qāḏar (to be dark/gloomy; used in 1 Kings 18:45 of the sky blackening; applied to mourning dress in Psalms 35:14; Job 42:9); ḥammāh = the sun (a poetic word, the warm/heat-giver). Mourning without the sun's comfort. ʾāqûm baqāhāl ʾăšawwēaʿ — 'I stand in the assembly and cry out' — qāhāl (the formal assembly of the community); šāwaʿ (to cry for help, the most urgent help-cry). Job's public, communal, unanswered cry for help.
29: ʾāḥ hāyîṯî lĕṯannîm — 'I have become a brother to jackals' — ʾāḥ (brother) with tannîm (jackals, or possibly sea-monsters; in Isaiah 13:21-22 and Job 34:13-15, jackals and ostriches inhabit desolate ruins, emblems of Edom's judgment). wĕrēʿa lĕḇnôṯ yaʿănāh — 'and a companion to ostriches' — rēaʿ (friend/companion); yaʿănāh (ostrich or possibly the screech-owl; its mournful cry makes it a wilderness-mourning symbol in Micah 1:8; Lamentations 4:3). The social companionship of the desolate creatures replaces human fellowship.
30: ʿôrî šāḥar mēʿālay — 'my skin has turned black on me' — šāḥar (to be dark; the same word as 'dawn' but here applied to skin darkening through disease or sun-exposure); the irony of using the dawn-word for skin-blackening. wĕʿaṣmî-ḥārāh miqqaḏḏāḥaṯ — 'and my bones burn with fever' — ḥārāh (to burn/be hot); qaddaḥat (fever/inflammation, also in Leviticus 26:16; Deuteronomy 28:22 as a covenant-curse); Job's body manifesting the covenant-curse vocabulary.
31: wayhî lĕʾēḇel kinnōrî — 'my lyre has become mourning' — kinnôr (the stringed instrument, David's instrument and the instrument of praise in Psalms 33:2; Job 43:4; Job 150:3); turned to ʾēḇel (mourning, the term for the formal mourning period; Genesis 27:41; Amos 5:16). wĕʿûgāḇî lĕqôl bōḵîm — 'and my flute to the sound of weeping' — ʿûgāḇ (a wind instrument, probably the flute; Genesis 4:21 lists it as Jubal's instrument alongside the kinnor); bōḵîm (weeping ones). The chapter closes with the reversal of musical praise into mourning lamentation — the full range of cultural expression now in the service of grief.
